title: MATH 3190 Homework 3 Solutions
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_3 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (11 points)

Part a (9 points)
Suppose we are attempting to predict a person’s ability to run a marathon in under 4 hours (coded as a 1) based on a number of factors: age, sex, BMI, and blood pressure. Below is the confusion matrix in this situation:




Actual: 1
Actual: 0




Predicted: 1
58
102


Predicted: 0
37
217



Find each of the following. Use proper formatting in Quarto when you type your answers. You can put equations between dollar signs ($$) and you can use the \frac{}{} (for a small fraction) or \dfrac{}{} (for a larger one) commands to nicely type fractions.

The prevalence of those that can run a mile under 4 hours.
The overall accuracy of these predictions.
The sensitivity (recall).
The specificity.
The positive predictive value (precision).
The negative predictive value.
The balanced accuracy.
Cohen’s Kappa (\(\kappa\)). Check out this link on Wikipedia: Cohen’s Kappa and scroll down to the section entitled Binary classification confusion matrix.
F1 score. Check out what this is on Wikipedia: F-Score

Answers
Prevalence: \(\dfrac{58+37}{58+37+102+217}=\dfrac{95}{414}=0.2295\).
Accuracy: \(\dfrac{58+217}{58+37+102+217}=\dfrac{275}{414} = 0.6643\).
Sensitivity: \(\dfrac{58}{58+37}=\dfrac{58}{95} = 0.6105\).
Specificity: \(\dfrac{217}{217+102}=\dfrac{217}{319} = 0.6803\).
Positive predictive value: \(\dfrac{58}{58+102}=\dfrac{58}{160} = 0.3625\).
Negative predictive value: \(\dfrac{217}{217+37}=\dfrac{217}{254} = 0.8543\).
Balanced accuracy: \(\dfrac{0.6105 + 0.6803}{2} = 0.6454\).
Cohen’s Kappa: \(\dfrac{2\times(58*217 - 37*102)}{(58+102)\times(102+217) + (58+37)\times(37+217)}=\dfrac{17624}{75170}=  0.2345\).
F1 score: \(\dfrac{2\times58}{2\times58+102+37}=0.4549\).


Part b (2 points)
Read more of the Wikipedia article on Cohen’s Kappa, especially the Interpreting magnitude and the Limitations part. I cannot really verify that you did this, so this is on your honor.



Problem 2 (79 points)
The adult dataset (from the UC Irvine database) is one that is used to predict whether a person makes over $50K a year based on some other variables. The data came from the Census Bureau in 1994 and can be found in the Data folder in my Math3190_S26 GitHub repo. More info on the dataset can be found in the “adult.names” file.

Part a (4 points)
Read the data into R as a tibble, change the column names to be descriptive about what the variable in that column is, and change the one containing salary information to a factor. Read the “adult.names” file to see the column names. Note that even though this file has a .data. suffix, it is comma separated, so you can read it in using read.csv() in base R and the convert it to a tibble or read_csv() from the tidyverse, which wil read it in as a tibble.

library(tidyverse) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
library(caret) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
# Set the working directory. 
# You'd have to change this to where you keep your data
setwd("~/My Drive/Math 3190 - Fundamentals of Data Science/Spring 2026/Data") 
adult <- read_csv("adult.data", col_names = F) |>
  mutate(X15 = factor(X15))
  
colnames(adult) <- c(
  "age",
  "workclass",
  "fnlwgt",
  "education",
  "education_num",
  "marital_status",
  "occupation",
  "relationship",
  "race",
  "sex",
  "capital_gain",
  "capital_loss",
  "hours_per_week",
  "native_country",
  "salary"
)



Part b (4 points)
Randomly split the dataset into a training and a testing group. Let’s use 4/5 of it for training and 1/5 for testing. You can do this with any function you’d like. Please set a seed with the set.seed() function before you do this so the results are reproducible.

library(caret) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
set.seed(2026)
test_index <- createDataPartition(adult$salary, times = 1, p = 0.20, list = F)

adult_train <- adult[-test_index,]
adult_test <- adult[test_index,]



Part c (5 points)
Fit two models for predicting whether a person’s salary is above $50K or not:
For the first model, fit a logistic regression model using something like glm(salary ~ age + education + race + sex + hours_per_week, data = adult_train, family = "binomial"). By the way, if you are unfamiliar with logistic regression, take some time to research it online or in my notes from Math 3150, which you can find here. We will deal with logistic regression again on the next homework. You don’t need to know everything about it, just learn a little about how it is used to predict the class for an observation. Of course, I’m happy to explain how it works in office hours as well.
For the second model, fit a \(k\) nearest neighbors model with \(k=7\) neighbors using the knn3() function in the caret package. Again, use age, education, race, sex, and hours_per_week as the predictors.

adult_train_glm <- glm(salary ~ age + education + race + sex + hours_per_week, 
                       data = adult_train, family = "binomial")

adult_train_knn <- knn3(salary ~ age + education + race + sex + hours_per_week, 
                        data = adult_train, k = 7)



Part d (5 points)
With logistic regression, the most common cutoff value for the predicted probability for predicting a “success” is 0.5. Using 0.5 as this cutoff (above 0.5 should be labeled as “>50K”), obtain the class predictions and convert the variable to a factor. You can use the predict() function with type = "response" to obtain the predicted probabilities of being in the “>50K” group and then compare those probabilities to 0.5. Then use the confusionMatrix() function in the caret package to obtain the confusion matrix and many associated statistics. Print all of the output from that function. Remember to use the positive argument in that function. The positive should be the >50K category.

y_hat_glm <- ifelse(predict(adult_train_glm, type = "response") > 0.5, 
                    ">50K", "<=50K") |>
  factor()
confusionMatrix(y_hat_glm, adult_train$salary, positive = ">50K")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction <=50K  >50K
     <=50K 27842  5889
     >50K   1882  3460
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.8011          
                 95% CI : (0.7971, 0.8051)
    No Information Rate : 0.7607          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.3596          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.37009         
            Specificity : 0.93668         
         Pos Pred Value : 0.64770         
         Neg Pred Value : 0.82541         
             Prevalence : 0.23927         
         Detection Rate : 0.08855         
   Detection Prevalence : 0.13672         
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.65339         
                                          
       'Positive' Class : >50K            
                                          




Part e (4 points)
Obtain the class predictions for your kNN model and output the results of the confusionMatrix() function for this. Note that it will take a few seconds to obtain the predictions for the kNN model.

y_hat_knn <- predict(adult_train_knn, adult_train, type = "class")
confusionMatrix(y_hat_knn, adult_train$salary, positive = ">50K")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction <=50K  >50K
     <=50K 27547  5188
     >50K   2177  4161
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.8115          
                 95% CI : (0.8076, 0.8154)
    No Information Rate : 0.7607          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.418           
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.4451          
            Specificity : 0.9268          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.6565          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.8415          
             Prevalence : 0.2393          
         Detection Rate : 0.1065          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.1622          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.6859          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : >50K            
                                          




Part f (4 points)
Using the output from parts d and e, write a few sentences comparing and contrasting the strengths and weaknesses of each model when it comes to predictions.
Answer The kNN model was better in nearly every metric. The only metric that was better for my logistic model was the specificity, which was 0.9367 for the logistic and 0.9268 for the kNN.


Part g (8 points)
Using the train() function in the caret package, perform 5-fold cross validation for \(k\) in the kNN model using only the training set and again using age, education, race, sex, and hours_per_week as the predictors. Set the search for \(k\) to be from 1 to 21 (we’ll stop at 21 to save time) in the tuneGrid argument. Make sure to use the trControl option to set it to cross validation. Then use Cohen’s \(\kappa\) as the metric to determine the best \(k\) value. You can change the metric in the train() function to Cohen’s \(\kappa\) by putting in metric = "Kappa".
Then, if the best \(k\) is different than 7, fit another kNN model with the optimal \(k\) value. Please set a seed at the beginning of this code chunk.
It is fairly computationally expensive to optimize the \(k\) for the kNN model here since it takes so long to obtain the predictions. So, this may take a few minutes to run. Note that you can add #| cache: true to the top of the code chunk to cache the output of the code so the code doesn’t need to run each time you render the document.

set.seed(2026)
knn_training <- train(salary ~ age + education + race + sex + hours_per_week,
      data = adult_train,
      method = "knn",
      metric = "Kappa",
      trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 5),
      tuneGrid = data.frame(k = seq(1, 21, 2)))
knn_training

k-Nearest Neighbors 

39073 samples
    5 predictor
    2 classes: '<=50K', '>50K' 

No pre-processing
Resampling: Cross-Validated (5 fold) 
Summary of sample sizes: 31260, 31258, 31258, 31258, 31258 
Resampling results across tuning parameters:

  k   Accuracy   Kappa    
   1  0.7741151  0.3329232
   3  0.7819976  0.3372468
   5  0.7825862  0.3322545
   7  0.7849152  0.3323791
   9  0.7851457  0.3272247
  11  0.7858879  0.3260877
  13  0.7849665  0.3192587
  15  0.7847107  0.3153935
  17  0.7845827  0.3124795
  19  0.7844035  0.3102072
  21  0.7840964  0.3086266

Kappa was used to select the optimal model using the largest value.
The final value used for the model was k = 3.

adult_train_knn3 <- knn3(salary ~ age + education + race + sex + hours_per_week, 
                         data = adult_train, k = 3)

The best k was 3 here according to kappa, so a new model was fit. Your k that maximizes kappa may be different due to randomness.


Part h (20 points)
We mentioned the most common cutoff value for the predicted probability for predicting a “success” in logistic regression is 0.5. However, we can adjust this value to make it easier or more difficult to predict a success. Let’s optimize this cutoff value using 5-fold cross validation. Note: we could also do this with kNN, but we will not on this assignment.
Using the cutoff values from 0.15 to 0.85 by 0.05 (0.15, 0.20, 0.25, and so on up to 0.85) for predicting whether an adult has a salary above 50K, find which one performs best on the training set using the metric of Cohen’s \(\kappa\), which is given in the output of the confusionMatrix() function. You will need a couple loops here since the train() function cannot do this for us. Note: you can find the indices of the rows in each fold using the createFolds() function in the caret library. Please set a seed at the beginning of your code chunk for this part.

set.seed(2026)
cutoffs <- seq(0.15, 0.85, by = 0.05)
split_index <- createFolds(adult_train$salary, k = 5)
metric <- matrix(rep(0, 15 * 5), nrow = 15)
rownames(metric) <- cutoffs
j <- 1
for(cutoff in cutoffs) {
  for(i in 1:5) {
    adult_glm_fold <- glm(salary ~ age + education + 
                       race + sex + hours_per_week, 
                       data = adult_train[-split_index[[i]],], 
                       family = "binomial")
    
    y_hat_glm <- ifelse(predict(adult_glm_fold,
                                adult_train[split_index[[i]],],
                                type = "response") > cutoff, 
                       ">50K", "<=50K") |>
      factor()
    
    metric[j, i] <- confusionMatrix(
      y_hat_glm, adult_train[split_index[[i]],]$salary
      )$overall[["Kappa"]]
  }
j <- j + 1
}
rowMeans(metric)

      0.15        0.2       0.25        0.3       0.35        0.4       0.45 
0.31346874 0.36935090 0.40074598 0.41154297 0.41803915 0.40979163 0.38821150 
       0.5       0.55        0.6       0.65        0.7       0.75        0.8 
0.35961308 0.31898739 0.27633682 0.23342100 0.18131791 0.12562916 0.07796742 
      0.85 
0.03727999 

best_cutoff <- cutoffs[which.max(rowMeans(metric))]
best_cutoff

[1] 0.35


The best cutoff here is 0.35 for me.


Part i (5 points)
Once you have your “optimal” cutoff value, repeat part d using this cutoff and compare the results of this output to the results of the output for a kNN model with the optimal \(k\) value you found in part g. For which statistics is the logistic regression better now and for which is it worse?

y_hat_glm2 <- ifelse(predict(adult_train_glm, type = "response") > 0.35, 
                    ">50K", "<=50K") |>
  factor()
confusionMatrix(y_hat_glm2, adult_train$salary, positive = ">50K")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction <=50K  >50K
     <=50K 25253  3956
     >50K   4471  5393
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.7843          
                 95% CI : (0.7802, 0.7884)
    No Information Rate : 0.7607          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.4185          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 2.153e-08       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.5769          
            Specificity : 0.8496          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.5467          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.8646          
             Prevalence : 0.2393          
         Detection Rate : 0.1380          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.2525          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.7132          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : >50K            
                                          

y_hat_knn3 <- predict(adult_train_knn3, newdata = adult_train, type = "class")
confusionMatrix(y_hat_knn3, adult_train$salary, positive = ">50K")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction <=50K  >50K
     <=50K 27596  4687
     >50K   2128  4662
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.8256          
                 95% CI : (0.8218, 0.8293)
    No Information Rate : 0.7607          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.4713          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.4987          
            Specificity : 0.9284          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.6866          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.8548          
             Prevalence : 0.2393          
         Detection Rate : 0.1193          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.1738          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.7135          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : >50K            
                                          


Answer
Now the logistic model is better when it comes to sensitivity, negative predictive value, detection rate, detection prevalence, and is very similar (though slightly lower) in balanced accuracy. The kNN model is better for the others.


Part j (15 points)
Finally, let’s test our two models (the logistic model with the “optimal” cutoff and the kNN model with the “optimal” \(k\)) on the test set. We must keep a few things in mind:

We must use the exact models we fit to the training set. We aren’t fitting new models here, we are just getting predictions from the test set using these models we already fit. You fit the logistic regression model in part c (with the best cutoff probability determined in part h) and you fit the kNN model in either part c or part g.
We should not use the results of the testing predictions to change our models. That should have been done with the training sets.

Find the predictions for the test set using both models, print the output of the confusionMatrix() function for each model, and compare the results in a few sentences.

library(caret) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
y_hat_glm_test <- ifelse(
  predict(adult_train_glm, adult_test, type = "response") > 0.35, 
  ">50K", 
  "<=50K"
  ) |>
  factor()
confusionMatrix(y_hat_glm_test, adult_test$salary, positive = ">50K")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction <=50K >50K
     <=50K  6368  993
     >50K   1063 1345
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.7895          
                 95% CI : (0.7813, 0.7976)
    No Information Rate : 0.7607          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 6.402e-12       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.4278          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.1281          
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.5753          
            Specificity : 0.8570          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.5586          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.8651          
             Prevalence : 0.2393          
         Detection Rate : 0.1377          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.2465          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.7161          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : >50K            
                                          

y_hat_knn_test <- predict(adult_train_knn3, adult_test, type = "class")
confusionMatrix(y_hat_knn_test, adult_test$salary, positive = ">50K")

Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction <=50K >50K
     <=50K  6717 1365
     >50K    714  973
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.7872          
                 95% CI : (0.7789, 0.7953)
    No Information Rate : 0.7607          
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 2.655e-10       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.3538          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.4162          
            Specificity : 0.9039          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.5768          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.8311          
             Prevalence : 0.2393          
         Detection Rate : 0.0996          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.1727          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.6600          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : >50K            
                                          


Answer
It seems like the logistic model is overall a little better. It has better overall accuracy, kappa, sensitivity, negative predictive value, detection rate, detection prevalence, and balanced accuracy, though many of those are close.
kNN is better when it comes to specificity and positive predictive value


Part k (5 points)
Even though one method may be better on a given dataset than another, that does not mean that method will always predict better. However, logistic regression has a few advantages over kNN regardless of predictive power. List at least three advantages logistic regression has over kNN.
Answer

A logistic regression model is quicker to fit and find predictions for.
Logistic regression has less of a tendency to over fit. This can be a problem for small values of \(k\) for kNN.
Logistic regression has slope values for the predictors, so we can interpret how each predictor is influencing the predicted probability. This is a huge advantage!

title: MATH 3190 Homework 1 Solutions
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_1 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

Problem 1 (18 points)
In two different code chunks, write two functions called gghist and ggbox. The first code chunk is already given below, but you’ll need to add the second one. The gghist function should create a ggplot histogram of a variable that is given as a vector. The ggbox function should create a ggplot box plot when a single numeric vector is given or it should create side-by-side box plots if one numeric and one categorical variables are given. You can check if a vector is numeric with the is.numeric() function. Allow the user to indicate whether it should be horizontal or vertical box plots. Make sure you set echo and eval both to true for these code chunks. Also make sure to label the code chunks.

gghist <- function(x, fill_color = "lightblue", num_bins = 30) {
  data.frame(x) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = x)) +
    geom_histogram(color = "black",
                   fill = fill_color,
                   bins = num_bins)
}


ggbox <- function(x, y = NULL, fill_color = "lightblue", horizontal = FALSE) {
  if(!is.numeric(x) & is.null(y)) {
    stop("The variable should be numeric.")
  }
  if(is.numeric(x) & is.null(y)) {
    if(horizontal) {
      p <- data.frame(x) |>
        ggplot(aes(x = x)) +
        geom_boxplot(color = "black",
                       fill = fill_color)
    } else {
      p <- data.frame(x) |>
        ggplot(aes(y = x)) +
        geom_boxplot(color = "black",
                       fill = fill_color)
    }
  } else if (is.numeric(x) & !is.numeric(y)) {
    if(horizontal) {
      p <- data.frame(x, y) |>
        ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) +
        geom_boxplot(color = "black",
                       fill = fill_color) +
        labs(x = "x", y = "y")
    } else {
      p <- data.frame(x, y) |>
        ggplot(aes(x = y, y = x)) +
        geom_boxplot(color = "black",
                       fill = fill_color) +
        labs(y = "x", x = "y")
    }
  } else if (!is.numeric(x) & is.numeric(y)) {
    if(horizontal) {
      p <- data.frame(x, y) |>
        ggplot(aes(x = y, y = x)) +
        geom_boxplot(color = "black",
                       fill = fill_color) +
        labs(x = "x", y = "y")
    } else {
      p <- data.frame(x, y) |>
        ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) +
        geom_boxplot(color = "black",
                       fill = fill_color) +
        labs(y = "x", x = "y")
    }
  }
  print(p)
}



Problem 2 (57 points)

Part a (6 points)
Learn about the built-in read.fwf() R function for use in downloading data from a URL into R. The widths and strip.white options will be especially useful here. Use this function to download the scores for all college basketball games for the 2024-2025 season http://kenpom.com/cbbga26.txt and then convert it to a tibble (load the tidyverse package first). The second team listed per line is the home team. It is not clear what the numbers, letters, or city names indicate after the second listed score. Notice that this is a “live” file that gets updated every day! So, your tibble size may change if you work on this assignment over the course of several days. That’s fine. Give the code you used to download these data in a labeled code chunk.

library(tidyverse) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
cbbga <- read.fwf("https://kenpom.com/cbbga26.txt", 
                    widths = c(10, 24, 3, 24, 3, 50),
                    strip.white = T) |>
  as_tibble()

Now lets practice using our tidy data/tidyverse tools! Using your cbbga tibble, try doing the following. In each part, save the object as a new tibble (like cbbga2, cbbga3, etc) and in each subsequent part, use the tibble you created in the previous part.


Part b (2 points)
Use rename() to rename all of your variables to names that make sense. Then print the first six rows of the new tibble using the head() function.

cbbga2 <- rename(cbbga, 
       date = V1, away_team = V2, away_score = V3, 
       home_team = V4, home_score = V5, notes = V6)
head(cbbga2)

# A tibble: 6 × 6
  date       away_team     away_score home_team         home_score notes        
  <chr>      <chr>              <int> <chr>                  <int> <chr>        
1 11/03/2025 McMurry               55 Abilene Christian         92 ""           
2 11/03/2025 James Madison         71 Akron                     85 ""           
3 11/03/2025 North Dakota          62 Alabama                   91 ""           
4 11/03/2025 Blue Mountain         60 Alabama A&M               80 ""           
5 11/03/2025 Florida               87 Arizona                   93 "N   Las Veg…
6 11/03/2025 Southern              77 Arkansas                 109 ""           




Part c (2 points)
Use mutate() to create a new column that gives the score differences (home team score minus away team score). Then print the first six rows of the new tibble using the head() function.

cbbga3 <- mutate(cbbga2, score_diff = home_score - away_score)
head(cbbga3)

# A tibble: 6 × 7
  date       away_team     away_score home_team      home_score notes score_diff
  <chr>      <chr>              <int> <chr>               <int> <chr>      <int>
1 11/03/2025 McMurry               55 Abilene Chris…         92 ""            37
2 11/03/2025 James Madison         71 Akron                  85 ""            14
3 11/03/2025 North Dakota          62 Alabama                91 ""            29
4 11/03/2025 Blue Mountain         60 Alabama A&M            80 ""            20
5 11/03/2025 Florida               87 Arizona                93 "N  …          6
6 11/03/2025 Southern              77 Arkansas              109 ""            32




Part d (2 points)
Use arrange() to sort the data set by the home team alphabetically. Then print the first six rows of the new tibble using the head() function.

cbbga4 <- mutate(cbbga3, home_team)
head(cbbga4)

# A tibble: 6 × 7
  date       away_team     away_score home_team      home_score notes score_diff
  <chr>      <chr>              <int> <chr>               <int> <chr>      <int>
1 11/03/2025 McMurry               55 Abilene Chris…         92 ""            37
2 11/03/2025 James Madison         71 Akron                  85 ""            14
3 11/03/2025 North Dakota          62 Alabama                91 ""            29
4 11/03/2025 Blue Mountain         60 Alabama A&M            80 ""            20
5 11/03/2025 Florida               87 Arizona                93 "N  …          6
6 11/03/2025 Southern              77 Arkansas              109 ""            32




Part e (2 points)
Use select() to remove the extra variable(s) that had that irrelevant information at the end of each line. Note: you can select every variable except one by using !. Then print the first six rows of the new tibble using the head() function.

cbbga5 <- select(cbbga4, !notes) # Get rid of that original V6 variable
head(cbbga5)

# A tibble: 6 × 6
  date       away_team     away_score home_team         home_score score_diff
  <chr>      <chr>              <int> <chr>                  <int>      <int>
1 11/03/2025 McMurry               55 Abilene Christian         92         37
2 11/03/2025 James Madison         71 Akron                     85         14
3 11/03/2025 North Dakota          62 Alabama                   91         29
4 11/03/2025 Blue Mountain         60 Alabama A&M               80         20
5 11/03/2025 Florida               87 Arizona                   93          6
6 11/03/2025 Southern              77 Arkansas                 109         32




Part f (3 points)
Put parts a-e all together in one piping expression (with 5 pipes) and save this as a new object in R.

cbbga26 <- read.fwf("https://kenpom.com/cbbga26.txt", 
                    widths = c(10, 24, 3, 24, 3, 50),
                    strip.white = T) |>
  as_tibble() |>
  rename(date = V1, away_team = V2, away_score = V3, 
         home_team = V4, home_score = V5, notes = V6) |>
  mutate(score_diff = home_score - away_score) |>
  arrange(home_team) |>
  select(!notes)
head(cbbga26)

# A tibble: 6 × 6
  date       away_team              away_score home_team   home_score score_diff
  <chr>      <chr>                       <int> <chr>            <int>      <int>
1 11/03/2025 McMurry                        55 Abilene Ch…         92         37
2 11/06/2025 Nebraska Omaha                 71 Abilene Ch…         73          2
3 11/11/2025 Southwestern Adventist         63 Abilene Ch…        104         41
4 11/25/2025 William & Mary                 92 Abilene Ch…         58        -34
5 11/28/2025 Jarvis Christian               55 Abilene Ch…         87         32
6 12/06/2025 New Mexico St.                 69 Abilene Ch…         77          8




Part g (3 points)
Use filter() to reduce the data down to only games played in 2025 (you could use the lubridate package for this, since it specializes in dealing with dates, but some base R packages will also work). Save this in a new tibble. We will use this tibble with only the 2025 years from here on out.

cbbga25 <- cbbga26 |>
  filter(substr(date, nchar(date)-3, nchar(date)) == "2025")

# Alternative:
cbbga25 <- cbbga26 |>
  filter(year(mdy(date)) == 2025)

head(cbbga25)

# A tibble: 6 × 6
  date       away_team              away_score home_team   home_score score_diff
  <chr>      <chr>                       <int> <chr>            <int>      <int>
1 11/03/2025 McMurry                        55 Abilene Ch…         92         37
2 11/06/2025 Nebraska Omaha                 71 Abilene Ch…         73          2
3 11/11/2025 Southwestern Adventist         63 Abilene Ch…        104         41
4 11/25/2025 William & Mary                 92 Abilene Ch…         58        -34
5 11/28/2025 Jarvis Christian               55 Abilene Ch…         87         32
6 12/06/2025 New Mexico St.                 69 Abilene Ch…         77          8




Part h (4 points)
Write a function that will filter the tibble to only games played by a given team. Demonstrate your function by displaying games played by SUU in 2025.

filter_bb <- function(bb_df, team_name) {
  filtered_df <- bb_df |>
    filter(home_team == team_name | away_team == team_name)
  filtered_df
}
filter_bb(cbbga25, "Southern Utah")

# A tibble: 14 × 6
   date       away_team            away_score home_team    home_score score_diff
   <chr>      <chr>                     <int> <chr>             <int>      <int>
 1 11/04/2025 Southern Utah                64 Arizona St.          81         17
 2 11/17/2025 Southern Utah                50 Gonzaga             122         72
 3 11/15/2025 Southern Utah                85 Nebraska Om…         90          5
 4 12/18/2025 Southern Utah                57 Northern Ar…         65          8
 5 12/06/2025 Southern Utah                70 Oregon St.           81         11
 6 11/28/2025 Southern Utah                54 Robert Morr…         61          7
 7 11/08/2025 UT Rio Grande Valley         95 Southern Ut…         72        -23
 8 11/11/2025 Bethesda                     60 Southern Ut…        118         58
 9 11/22/2025 Nobel                        68 Southern Ut…        103         35
10 12/01/2025 West Coast Baptist           59 Southern Ut…        124         65
11 11/29/2025 Southern Utah                70 Stetson              68         -2
12 12/29/2025 Southern Utah                66 Utah Tech            80         14
13 12/13/2025 Southern Utah                69 Washington          105         36
14 11/19/2025 Southern Utah                74 Washington …         98         24




Part i (7 points)
Use summarize() to extract SUU’s win/loss record and winning percentage for their 2025 games. Hint: using the case_when() function inside of a mutate() function to create a new variable that indicates whether SUU won or lost is helpful. You can verify on the Internet that SUU had 4 wins and 10 losses in 2025, so if your code retruns those values, you did it correctly.

cbbga25 |>
  filter(home_team == "Southern Utah" | away_team == "Southern Utah") |>
  mutate("W/L" = case_when(
    home_team == "Southern Utah" & score_diff > 0 ~ "W",
    away_team == "Southern Utah" & score_diff < 0 ~ "W",
    .default = "L")) |>
  summarize(wins = sum(`W/L` == "W"),
            losses = sum(`W/L` == "L"),
            winning_perc = round(sum(`W/L` == "W")/n()*100, 2))

# A tibble: 1 × 3
   wins losses winning_perc
  <int>  <int>        <dbl>
1     4     10         28.6




Part j (7 points)
Generalize this by writing a function that will do this for a given team, and create a tibble with this information for all teams. Arrange this tibble first by winning percentage (descending) and by team (alphabetically) if there are any ties when it comes to winning percentage. A for loop and the add_row() function may be useful here.

win_loss_bb <- function(team_name, bb_df) {
  bb_df |>
    filter(home_team == team_name | away_team == team_name) |>
    mutate("W/L" = case_when(
      home_team == team_name & score_diff > 0 ~ "W",
      away_team == team_name & score_diff < 0 ~ "W",
      .default = "L")) |>
    summarize(team = team_name,
              wins = sum(`W/L` == "W"),
              losses = sum(`W/L` == "L"),
              winning_perc = round(sum(`W/L` == "W")/n()*100, 2))
}
win_loss_bb("Southern Utah", cbbga25)

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  team           wins losses winning_perc
  <chr>         <int>  <int>        <dbl>
1 Southern Utah     4     10         28.6

all_teams <- unique(c(cbbga25$home_team, cbbga25$away_team))
win_loss_tibble <- win_loss_bb(all_teams[1], cbbga25)
for(i in 2:length(all_teams)) {
  win_loss_tibble <- add_row(win_loss_tibble, 
                             win_loss_bb(all_teams[i], cbbga25))
}
win_loss_tibble <- win_loss_tibble |>
  arrange(desc(winning_perc), team)
win_loss_tibble

# A tibble: 724 × 4
   team           wins losses winning_perc
   <chr>         <int>  <int>        <dbl>
 1 Arizona          13      0          100
 2 Iowa St.         13      0          100
 3 Miami OH         14      0          100
 4 Michigan         12      0          100
 5 Montana Tech      1      0          100
 6 Nebraska         13      0          100
 7 Northwood MI      1      0          100
 8 Vanderbilt       13      0          100
 9 Virginia St.      1      0          100
10 Wilmington DE     1      0          100
# ℹ 714 more rows




Part k (5 points)
With only the 2025 games, use the gghist() you wrote in Problem 1 on the score differences variable you created in part c. Add appropriate labels and a title to your graph. Also, use the #| fig-width:, #| fig-height:, #| out-width:, and #| fig-align: code chunk options to center the figure and then to adjust your figure to a size you like. Play around with different sizes to see what those options adjust.

gghist(cbbga25$score_diff) + 
  labs(x = "Score Difference (Home - Away Team)", 
       title = "Histogram of Home Team and Away Team Scores")










Part l (9 points)
Filter the tibble with all 2025 games to only include Utah Tech or Southern Utah, then crate a tibble or data frame where each row is a game played by either team that has one column indicating the school, Utah Tech or Southern Utah, and a second column indicating the number of points that team played, regardless of whether the team was home or away. Then use your ggbox() function to create a side-by-side boxplot of number of points scored in all 2025 games for each team.
Note, this is a little challenging. The most straightforward way to do this is to use the pivot_longer() function with cols = the names of the two columns that have the away and the home teams (the names of these columns should not be in quotes, but they should be in a vector c()). Try to do this yourself first, but using AI for some help is fine.

cbbga_utah <- cbbga25 |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = c(away_team, home_team), values_to = "team") |>
  mutate(
    score = case_when(
      name == "away_team" ~ away_score,
      name == "home_team" ~ home_score
    )
  ) |> 
  filter(team %in% c("Southern Utah", "Utah Tech")) |>
  select(team, score)
ggbox(cbbga_utah$score, cbbga_utah$team)










Part m (5 points)
Create one more appropriate graphs for the 2025 basketball data. This graph could be anything you’d like as long as you use ggplot2, it shows something meaningful, and it is not the same as the graphs you made in parts k or l.
Answers will vary here.



Problem 3 (15 points)
Repeat parts b-e and g of Problem 2 using Python in Quarto. First, pass the original object that you read in from the website to Python without any changes to it (you do not need to read the file from the web in Python, but you can if you’d like) and then use pandas (or polars if you’d like) to rename the columns as indicated in part b, add the columns specified in part c, arrange the data as in part d, drop the “garbage” column as in part e, and filter it down as in part g. The pandas functions rename, assign (instead of mutate), drop (instead of select) and str.contains (used to select the right rows) will be useful here. Be sure to follow the guide in Notes 3 to properly install Python, install the pandas library and to load it in R.

library(reticulate)
# py_install(packages = "pandas") # Only run this once.


import pandas as pd
# Pass object as r.cbbga and rename the columns
cbbga26 = r.cbbga
cbbga26 = cbbga26.rename(
  columns = {"V1": "date", "V2": "away_team", "V3": "away_score",
             "V4": "home_team", "V5": "home_score", "V6": "notes"}
  )

# Add new columns
cbbga26 = cbbga26.assign(
    score_diff = cbbga26["home_score"]-cbbga26["away_score"]
    )
# Drop V6
cbbga26 = cbbga26.drop(columns = ["notes"])

# Filter down to 2025 scores:
cbbga25 = cbbga26[cbbga26["date"].str.contains("2025")]


# Same as above, but in one expression:
cbbga25 = r.cbbga.rename(
  columns = {"V1": "date", "V2": "away_team", "V3": "away_score",
             "V4": "home_team", "V5": "home_score", "V6": "notes"}
  ).assign(
    score_diff = lambda x: x["home_score"]-x["away_score"]
    ).drop(
      columns = ["notes"]
      ).query(
        "date.str.contains('2025')"
        )
print(cbbga25)

            date      away_team  ...  home_score score_diff
0     11/03/2025        McMurry  ...          92         37
1     11/03/2025  James Madison  ...          85         14
2     11/03/2025   North Dakota  ...          91         29
3     11/03/2025  Blue Mountain  ...          80         20
4     11/03/2025        Florida  ...          93          6
...          ...            ...  ...         ...        ...
2741  12/31/2025         DePaul  ...          71          5
2742  12/31/2025       Virginia  ...          95         10
2743  12/31/2025        Wofford  ...          74         -5
2744  12/31/2025    Stony Brook  ...          76         19
2745  12/31/2025    Connecticut  ...          67        -23

You will `turn in’ this homework by uploading to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the “Labs/Lab_4” directory.

href: Lab 4 - R Shiny/Lab 4 - R Shiny R.html#problem-1---k-means-tutorial
title: MATH 3190 Lab 4
MATH 3190 Lab 4
text: Now its time to practice what we have learned in class and learn even more! Note that this lab should be completed in R Markdown or Quarto and Knitted to an html document. You will `turn in’ this homework by uploading to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the “Labs/Lab_4” directory.

title: MATH 3190 Lab 4
MATH 3190 Lab 3
text:
Now its time to practice what we have learned in class and learn even more! Note that this lab should be completed in R Markdown or Quarto and Knitted to an html document. You will `turn in’ this homework by uploading to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the “Labs/Lab_3” directory.

Problem 1 - Mammal Sleep
In the ggplot2 library, there is a tibble called msleep. You can load this by loading the ggplot2 library (type library(ggplot2)). From there, you can work with the msleep tibble.

Part a
Below is some code for creating a scatter plot of body weight vs brain weight:

ggplot(msleep, aes(x = brainwt, y = bodywt)) +
  geom_point()

Warning: Removed 27 rows containing missing values or values
outside the scale range (`geom_point()`).









In the code chunk below, modify this code so that it is a line plot instead of a scatter plot.


Part b
Create a new code chunk below this paragraph and modify the code from part (a) so that both the points and the line are plotted. Change the color of the line and change the color of the points to any color you’d like. Note: you can add on more than one geom function.


Part c
Clearly, it is difficult to see all the points on the plot since many are bunched together. We can alleviate this by plotting the on the log (base 10) scale. Using tidyverse functions, define a new data frame (call it msleep2) with two new variables: one that is the log of brainwt and one that is the log of bodywt. You can just use the log10() function to actually take the log of those variables. Then insert a code chuck below that produces a scatter plot with the log of body weight on the y-axis and the log of brain weight on the x-axis. Be sure the x and y axis labels to indicate that the variables are logged.


Part d
An alternative way to plot these data on the log scale is to plot the untransformed variables on the x and y axes and then adding on the functions scale_y_log10() and scale_x_log10() to your plot. Go ahead and do this in a code chunk below. Note that these functions will adjust the scale on the axes so that they correspond to the original variable scales, not the logged variable scales. Very nice!

Part e
Below is some code to produce a bar plot of the vore variable, which tells us whether the animal is a carnivore, omnivore or herbivore:

ggplot(msleep, aes(x = vore)) +
  geom_bar()








However, this bar plot is quite boring looking. In a code chunk below, make a bar plot of the conservation variable and make it more colorful. Feel free to spice up the bar plot for the vore variable above as well.



Part f
The following code will create a histogram for the awake variable, which tells us how long the animal stays awake per day (in hours):

ggplot(msleep, aes(x = awake)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10)








First, modify that code in the above code chunk so we can easily see each bar in the histogram. Use colors of your choice. Then, in a code chunk below, create a box plot instead of a histogram for the awake variable. Make the box plot look better with the color and fill options.


Part g
The following code will create box plots of the sleep_time variable split by conservation with the labels changed:

ggplot(msleep, aes(x = conservation, y = sleep_total, 
                   fill = conservation)) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  labs(x = "Conservation Status", 
       y = "Total Sleep Time ", 
       fill = "Conservation ")








In a new code chunk, create box plots of sleep_rem split by the vore variable. Change the labels to be appropriate. Also, explain what the fill option does here. How does this differ from putting the fill option in the geom_boxplot() function?


Part h
The following code will give density plots of the logged brain weight in the msleep2 data frame you created in part (c) split by conservation. If you called your variable something different than logbrain in part (c), you will have to adjust that here. This code also adjusts the labels, adds a title, and changes the font size. Note: I put eval = F in the R code chunk option because I did not create an msleep2 tibble, so this would not run.

ggplot(msleep2, aes(x = logbrain, fill = conservation)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) + 
  labs(x = "Log(Brain Weight)", y = "Density", 
       title = "Density Plots of Log(Brain Weight)") +
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 14))

Adjust this code to make density plots for logbody (that you also created in part (c)) split by vore. Adjust the labs() options to make them appropriate for this situation. Also, change the size of the axis labels to be 16 and make the title bold font.



Problem 2 - College Distance
College Distance. In the Data folder in the Math3190_Sp24 repo on my GitHub page is the data set CollegeDistance.csv that was collected in the 1980s on high school students. This data set contains the following variables:

gender: factor indicating gender.
ethnicity: factor indicating ethnicity (African-American, Hispanic or other).
score: base year composite test score. These are achievement tests given to high school seniors in the sample.
fcollege: factor. Is the father a college graduate?
mcollege: factor. Is the mother a college graduate?
home: factor. Does the family own their home?
urban: factor. Is the school in an urban area?
unemp: county unemployment rate in 1980.
wage: state hourly wage in manufacturing in 1980.
distance: distance from 4-year college (in 10 miles).
tuition: average state 4-year college tuition (in 1000 USD).
education: number of years of education.
income: factor. Is the family income above USD 25,000 per year?
region: factor indicating region (West or other).

For each part, insert an R code chunk that does what is asked.

Part a
Read this data set into R. Use a tidyverse function to read it in as a tibble rather than read.csv() or read.table().


Part b
Create a bar plot of the gender variable. Give it a title and change the y axis label to “Frequency”. Feel free to make the bars look nicer as well!


Part c
Although pie charts are inferior to bar charts in virtually every way, use this website, https://r-graph-gallery.com/piechart-ggplot2.html, to look up how to make a pie chart in ggplot2 and make one for the ethnicity variable. Some tidyverse functions like group_by(), and summarize() will be useful here. This is a bit more challenging, so feel free to come back to this one later.


Part d
Make a stacked bar chart for ethnicity split by income (one bar for each income) so that each bar has a height of 1. Adjust the labels so that they are “African American”, “Hispanic”, and “Other”. You can do this with the labels option in the scale_fill_manual() function. Then change the colors to be colorblind-friendly. One palette that is good for this is the following:

cbPalette <- c("#999999", "#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", 
               "#F0E442", "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7")

That palette above is good for up to 8 categories. You can use those colors by putting values = cbPalette in the scale_fill_manual() function.


Part e
Another way to easily change the palette is with the scale_fill_brewer() function. There are built-in palettes available. You can also change the fill legend labels with the labels option in this function. At this website: https://r-graph-gallery.com/38-rcolorbrewers-palettes.html, you can see the available palettes. Replace the scale_fill_manual() function from the previous part of this problem with the scale_fill_brewer() and use a palette that looks good to you.


Part f
Create a scatter plot with tuition on the y-axis and unemp on the x-axis. Change the x and y labels to be “Unemployment Rate” and “Average 4-Year College Tuition (in thousands)”. Change the plot so that the color of the points are based on the region. Then change the size of the labels to be 14 (the default size is 11) and put a title that is size 14 and bold. Finally, look up how to adjust where the tick marks and grid lines are on the y-axis and put space them every 0.25 thousand.


Part g
Create a histogram of the distance variable. Change the x-axis label so the units of distance are shown and add a title. Also, change the color from gray to one of your choice and put borders around the histogram bars. Finally, change the background theme to be anything but gray.


Part h
Create side-by-side box plots for the score variable split by income. Change the x-axis and y-axis labels and give the plot a title. Change the color of the box plots to one of your choice.


Part i
Create a bar plot of gender with bars split by ethnicity. Use position = "dodge" to get ggplot to put the bars side by side.



Problem 3 - Airlines
The file airlines.csv can also be found on my GitHub page. This data set contains information about 6 airlines from 6 years between 1970 to 1984. This includes the airline, the year, the cost (in $1,000), the output index number (in revenue passenger miles), the fuel price, and the average load factor of the fleet.

Part a
Read the data file into R using a tidyverse function. Change the airline variable from numeric to a factor using a pipe and the mutate() function.


Part b
Create a plot that looks as similar as possible to the one in the image below. Note, the range on the y-axis is from 0 to 5,000,000 and the color palette is that cbPalette we used earlier. The line type can be changed by putting geom_line(aes(linetype = airline)). The font size of the axis labels is 14 and the sizer of the title is 18.



Part c
Now, instead of plotting all six airlines on the same plot, use facet_grid() to graph six plots (one for each airline) side-by-side. Be sure to put labeller = label_both in the facet_grid() function so it is more readable.

title: Math 3190 Website - Rick Brown
text:
This is a website that will compile all assignments I do for Math 3190 - Fundamentals of Data Science at Southern Utah University in Spring 2026.
To learn more about Quarto websites visit https://quarto.org/docs/websites.

objectID: Lab 4 - R Shiny/Lab 4 - R Shiny App.html
href: Lab 4 - R Shiny/Lab 4 - R Shiny App.html
title: MATH 3190 Lab 4
text:
Step 09: Final App
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 600
library(shiny)
ui <- fluidPage(
  
  # Put a titlePanel here
  titlePanel("k-means clustering"),
  
  sidebarLayout(
    # Sidebar. Put your inputs inside the sidebarPanel
    sidebarPanel(
      selectInput('xcol', 'X Variable', names(iris)),
      selectInput('ycol', 'Y Variable', names(iris),
                  selected=names(iris)[[2]]),
      numericInput('clusters', 'Cluster count', 3,
                   min = 1, max = 9)
    ),
    
    # Main panel. put your output plot here
    mainPanel(
      plotOutput('plot1')
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  
  selectedData <- reactive({
    iris[, c(input$xcol, input$ycol)]
  })
  
  clusters <- reactive({
    kmeans(selectedData(), input$clusters)
  })
  
  output$plot1 <- renderPlot({
    palette(c("#E41A1C", "#377EB8", "#4DAF4A", "#984EA3",
              "#FF7F00", "#FFFF33", "#A65628", "#F781BF", "#999999"))
    
    par(mar = c(5.1, 4.1, 0, 1))
    plot(selectedData(),
         col = clusters()$cluster,
         pch = 20, cex = 3)
    points(clusters()$centers, pch = 4, cex = 4, lwd = 4)
  })
}

shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)


Problem 2 - R Shiny for mtcars
The mtcars data set was extracted from the 1974 Motor Trend US magazine, and comprises fuel consumption and 10 aspects of automobile design and performance for 32 automobiles (1973–74 models). This data set is already built into R. You can load it by typing data(mtcars). Once you do that, you should change the cyl, vs, am, gear, and carb variables to be factors rather than numeric.
Your job is to build an app that has the following:

A title for the app.
An input select box that lets the user specify which variable in the data set they are selecting.
An input select box that lets the user specify what type of plot to use, either a histogram, box plot, or bar plot.
The app should then output the plot of the variable that is selcted. The plot should have a nice color, a title, and easy to read axes.
If the variable is quantiative and a bar plot is selected, then a message should appear to the user indicating the wrong type of plot is selected.
Likewise, if the variable is categorical and a histogram or box plot is selected, then a message should appear to the user indicating the wrong type of plot is selected.

Some hints:

The app will need a ui element, a server function, and the last line should be shinyApp(server = server, ui = ui).
Add items to your app one at a time and run it often to make sure things are working as expected.
You’ll need both a renderText() and a renderPlot() function in the server.
if statements are very useful in these shiny apps.
The variable selected by the user will be saved as an object in input$. You should use the reactive() functions to save it as an object in the server function and then access it in the render...() functions by typing its name with parentheses () at the end. For example, if you save it as var_select then you will access it as var_select() in the reactive() functions.
In ggplot, we are used to calling the name of the variable we are plotting in the aes() function. However, the variable we are plotting is being chosen by the user and is reactive. You can access the variable you want by putting mtcars as the first argument in ggplot and then by putting aes(x = .data[[var_select()]]), if you saved your variable as var_select in the server function. That .data will refer back to whatever data set you entered into the ggplot function. So, it will look like ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = .data[[var_select()]])). Of course, you can change that x to an y where appropriate.

One more thing about R Markdown. If you change the R code chunk option from eval = F to eval = T after building your app, the code will run, but the app will not be interactive. It is just a static file. You can make it interaction by adding runtime: shiny (not indented) to the bottom of the YAML (under the editor_options: section). If you save the file, the Knit button at the top should change to a play button that says “Run Document”. If you click that, it will make the document interactive. You cannot save and share the output of document in this state, though.

library(tidyverse)
library(shiny)

title: MATH 3190 Homework 2 Solutions
text:
Note that your homework should be completed in Quarto. You can just add your answers and code to this document in the appropriate part. The file should be rendered as an html document or pdf document (html is quicker to render and you will need an installation of LaTeX to render as a pdf). You will “turn in” this homework by uploading the files, the .qmd and output file, to your GitHub Math_3190_Assignment repository in the Homework/Homework_2 directory. Make sure all supporting files (if there are any) are in this directory as well. You should use git and Unix commands in a terminal for this (i.e. don’t manually upload files to GitHub). In addition, you will submit just your html or pdf file on Canvas to unlock the solutions.
Don’t forget to also put a self-critique comment on Canvas indicating what you did correctly and incorrectly and include any questions you may have about the assignment after submitting the assignment and thoroughly looking through the solutions.

title: MATH 3190 Homework 2 Solutions
text:
Part e (11 points)
Many of these queries are generating outputs that are fairly small. That is, they are easily able to be stored as variables in R. A couple hundred thousand rows is fine to work with since that only takes a couple MB of RAM.

Using the dbGetQuery() function again, obtain the arrival delay times and the name of the airline (by joining with the airlines table) for all the data entries that have a delay time more than 60 minutes. Save this output as a tibble in R.
Once you have this, make side-by-side boxplots in ggplot() for the arrival delay time split by the airline. This plot won’t look too great unless the y-axis is scaled. Go ahead and use the log10 scale like we did back in Lab 2. Then look up how to angle the axis labels so they do not overlap and incorporate that in your plot.


library(tidyverse) |> suppressPackageStartupMessages()
late_flights <- dbGetQuery(flights_con,
           "SELECT 
            flights.ARRIVAL_DELAY, 
            airlines.AIRLINE AS AIRLINE_NAME
           FROM flights 
           JOIN airlines
            ON flights.AIRLINE = airlines.IATA_CODE
           WHERE ARRIVAL_DELAY > 60") |>
  tibble()


ggplot(late_flights, aes(x = AIRLINE_NAME, y = ARRIVAL_DELAY, fill = AIRLINE_NAME)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust=1),
        legend.position = "None") +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = "Airline", y = "Arrival Delay (More than 60 Minutes)")









Part f (10 points)

Now, let’s NOT use the dbGetQuery() function. Instead, insert an SQL code chunk for this part of the problem that selects the average arrival delay (for some reason, there is not a median function built in to SQL) and the airline’s full name for each airline. So, this output should only have 14 rows.
Then pass this output back to R and create a barplot with the airlines on the x-axis and the average arrival delay on the y-axis. Again, angle the x-axis labels so they do not overlap.


SELECT 
  AVG(flights.ARRIVAL_DELAY) AS 'Average Arrival Delay',
  airlines.AIRLINE AS 'Airline Name'
FROM flights 
JOIN airlines
  ON flights.AIRLINE = airlines.IATA_CODE
GROUP BY airlines.AIRLINE


ggplot(avg_delay, aes(x = `Airline Name`, 
                      y = `Average Arrival Delay`,
                      fill = `Airline Name`)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust=1),
        legend.position = "None")










Part g (12 points)

Now let’s use the dbplyr add-on to the dplyr package to work with an SQL database directly. You may have to install the dbplyr package. Use the tbl() function to create an object you can work with directly.
With that object, using R commands from the tidyverse, find the average and standard deviation of the elapsed time of the flights by airline and arrange it by the SD (with the largest at the top). Make sure to use the inner_join() function so that the name of the airline, not just its code, is given. Note that to save this object in R’s memory, you will need to do something like pipe it into the collect() function.
Finally, make a ggplot bar graph like the one you made in Part f, but use the standard deviation as the heights on the y-axis. Why do you suppose the airline with the largest standard deviation in elapsed time has such variable flight times?


flights <- tbl(flights_con, "flights")
airlines <- tbl(flights_con, "airlines")

elapsed_stats <- inner_join(flights, airlines, 
                            by = join_by(AIRLINE == IATA_CODE)) |>
  rename("Airline Name" = AIRLINE.y) |>
  group_by(`Airline Name`) |>
  summarize("Average Elapsed Time" = mean(ELAPSED_TIME, na.rm = T),
            "SD Elapsed Time" = sd(ELAPSED_TIME, na.rm = T)) |>
  arrange(desc(`SD Elapsed Time`)) |>
  collect()

ggplot(elapsed_stats, aes(x = `Airline Name`, 
                      y = `SD Elapsed Time`,
                      fill = `Airline Name`)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust=1),
        legend.position = "None")








The airline with the largest standard deviation is Hawaiian Airlines. This makes sense since Hawaiian airlines only has either very short flights from island to island or very long flights from Hawaii to the mainland.
Finally, when you are done with this assignment, it is good practice to close the connection to the flights database.

dbDisconnect(flights_con)
